candidate_problems.text: How can we non-circularly distinguish a genuinely new 'shared structural constraint' from a merely redescribed similarity after the fact? The conjecture says novelty lies in exposing a load-bearing organization, but it leaves open operational criteria for when a bridge is truly knowledge-generating rather than a retrospective narrative shell. A key unresolved tension is whether survivability under criticism, bidirectional fruitfulness, compression, predictive transfer, or counterfactual dependence are sufficient markers, and how to separate these from ordinary abstraction or relabeling.

What mechanism explains the claimed 'public good' underproduction of analogical bridges, and when is that the right model rather than simple ignorance, disciplinary isolation, or search-cost limits? The conjecture hinges on structural underprovision as distinct from mere confusion, but it does not yet specify the system structure—agents, incentives, feedback loops, institutions, and discovery costs—that would make valuable analogies systematically undersupplied. This raises the open problem of building a causal model that predicts where analogical knowledge should be rare, delayed, or overproduced.

What is the ontological and epistemic status of the 'new abstract object of knowledge' created by analogy, and how is it individuated? The conjecture treats the explanatory bridge as something real and novel, not reducible to either domain, but it remains unclear what makes two formulations the same bridge, how generalization boundaries are set, and when a proposed common structure defines a legitimate new problem-class rather than an overextended unification. This matters because without principled individuation, claims of novelty and progress become hard to test.
